stem: The nurse is reviewing the clinical record for a client receiving mechanical ventilation. Which intervention is the priority to prevent ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) based on the client's current status?

options.text: Elevate the head of the bed to 30-45 degrees.
Perform oral care with chlorhexidine every 8 hours.
Change the ventilator circuit tubing daily.

itemContext.sbar:
Admission Note (Day 1, 07:00)
S: Client admitted for acute respiratory failure secondary to a severe COPD exacerbation.
B: 68-year-old male with a history of COPD, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes. He was intubated in the emergency department at 07:00 due to worsening hypoxemia and hypercapnia, despite non-invasive ventilation.
A: Post-intubation, the client is sedated on a propofol infusion. He is hemodynamically stable and placed on assist-control ventilation. Initial vitals are stable. An arterial line was placed for close monitoring.
R: Continue sedation and mechanical ventilation. Obtain baseline labs, ABG, and a portable chest x-ray. Initiate the VAP prevention bundle per protocol.

Progress Note (Day 1, 12:00)
S: Client remains intubated and sedated. SpO2 has trended down to 88% on current settings.
B: Intubated for 5 hours. Last oral care was performed at 08:00.
A: Temperature is now 101.7°F (38.7°C). Tachycardic at 110 bpm. Thick, purulent, yellow secretions were suctioned at 11:30. The WBC is elevated at 14,500/mm³. The head of the bed is currently at 10 degrees. The chest x-ray shows early bilateral infiltrates.
R: Recommend obtaining a sputum culture. Continue close monitoring of vital signs and oxygenation. Reinforce all VAP bundle components, especially head of bed elevation.

tabs.title: Nurses' Notes
tabs.content:
Admission Note (Day 1, 07:00)
S: Client admitted for acute respiratory failure secondary to a severe COPD exacerbation.
B: 68-year-old male with a history of COPD, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes. He was intubated in the emergency department at 07:00 due to worsening hypoxemia and hypercapnia, despite non-invasive ventilation.
A: Post-intubation, the client is sedated on a propofol infusion. He is hemodynamically stable and placed on assist-control ventilation. Initial vitals are stable. An arterial line was placed for close monitoring.
R: Continue sedation and mechanical ventilation. Obtain baseline labs, ABG, and a portable chest x-ray. Initiate the VAP prevention bundle per protocol.

Progress Note (Day 1, 12:00)
S: Client remains intubated and sedated. SpO2 has trended down to 88% on current settings.
B: Intubated for 5 hours. Last oral care was performed at 08:00.
A: Temperature is now 101.7°F (38.7°C). Tachycardic at 110 bpm. Thick, purulent, yellow secretions were suctioned at 11:30. The WBC is elevated at 14,500/mm³. The head of the bed is currently at 10 degrees. The chest x-ray shows early bilateral infiltrates.
R: Recommend obtaining a sputum culture. Continue close monitoring of vital signs and oxygenation. Reinforce all VAP bundle components, especially head of bed elevation.

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
Time: 12:00
Respiratory: Coarse crackles auscultated bilaterally. Endotracheal tube in place, secured at 23 cm at the lip. Thick, purulent yellow secretions noted in tubing. Spontaneous respiratory effort present over set ventilator rate.
Cardiovascular: S1, S2 audible. Tachycardia with regular rhythm. Pulses 2+ peripherally.
Neurological: Sedated on continuous infusion, Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale (RASS) -4. Pupils equal and reactive to light.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content:
Type: Chest X-ray (Portable AP)
Time: 11:00
Findings: Endotracheal tube tip is appropriately positioned, 4 cm superior to the carina. Diffuse interstitial opacities are present bilaterally, more prominent in the right lower lobe, suggestive of developing airspace disease. Heart size is normal. No evidence of large pleural effusion or pneumothorax.
Impression: Early bilateral infiltrates, concerning for developing pneumonia.

content.Action: Mechanical Ventilation: AC/VC mode, FiO2 60%, TV 450 mL, RR 16, PEEP 5 cm H2O

rationale.correct: Elevating the head of the bed (HOB) to 30-45 degrees is a critical, evidence-based intervention to prevent VAP. This semi-recumbent position uses gravity to reduce the risk of gastroesophageal reflux and subsequent aspiration of oropharyngeal and gastric secretions into the lower respiratory tract. Given the client's purulent secretions and gram stain showing bacteria, preventing these pathogens from entering the lungs is the highest priority.
rationale.incorrect: Increasing the FiO2 treats the symptom of hypoxemia but does not address the underlying cause, which is likely a developing pneumonia. Prolonged high FiO2 levels can lead to oxygen toxicity and absorptive atelectasis, further damaging the lungs. Performing oral care every 8 hours is an unsafe interval; the standard is every 2-4 hours to effectively reduce bacterial biofilm. Changing the ventilator circuit daily is an outdated practice that increases the risk of introducing pathogens by breaking the closed system.

answerBreakdown.content: Correct. The nurse's notes indicate the HOB is at 10 degrees. The VAP bundle order requires 30-45 degrees. This simple, non-invasive action directly addresses the primary mechanism of VAP development: microaspiration of colonized secretions.

Incorrect. While the SpO2 is low (88%), this is a reactive measure, not a preventative one. The priority is to prevent the worsening of the infection that is causing the hypoxemia. This action fails to address the root cause.

Incorrect. This option suggests an unsafe frequency. The care plan orders oral care every 4 hours. Extending this to 8 hours would allow for significant bacterial colonization in the oropharynx, increasing the VAP risk.

Incorrect. This is contrary to current infection control guidelines. Ventilator circuits are changed only when visibly soiled or malfunctioning to maintain a closed system and minimize the risk of introducing external pathogens.

clinicalPearls: The 'VAP bundle' is a set of evidence-based practices that, when implemented together, significantly reduce the incidence of ventilator-associated pneumonia.
Early signs of VAP include new or worsening infiltrates on chest x-ray, fever, leukocytosis, and purulent secretions.
Daily sedation vacations and assessment of readiness to extubate are key components of VAP prevention, as they reduce the duration of mechanical ventilation, the single greatest risk factor.
rationale.questionTrap: The trap is choosing a seemingly urgent action, like increasing FiO2 to correct the low SpO2, over a fundamental preventative measure. Always prioritize interventions that prevent the problem over those that only manage a symptom, especially when the question asks about prevention.
rationale.mnemonic:
VAP Prevention: HOPES

H - Head of bed elevation (30-45 degrees)
O - Oral care (with chlorhexidine q2-4h)
P - Peptic ulcer disease prophylaxis
E - Early weaning/Extubation assessment (sedation vacations)
S - Subglottic suctioning
